text: The surgeon begins the intervention by performing medial control of the uterine vessels at their origin. The initial step involves opening the posterior phase of the broad ligaments.

The surgeon creates an incision in the large ligament to facilitate the 'champagne effect' of gas expansion, aiding in the development of the ligament. The visual field reveals two contiguous conduits: an external vascular conduit and an internal urinary conduit. The ureter is observed running parallel to the inner iliac artery in the retro-ligamentary portion.

The surgeon carefully rotates the urethra and gradually detaches it from the inner iliac artery using progressive movements. The internal iliac artery is visible, and as the dissection progresses from the caudal side, the uterine artery is identified. The urethra is clearly seen crossing the uterine artery, and a vessel detaching from the inner iliac artery is observed beating against the urethra.

Under visual control, the surgeon clips the uterine artery without risk. The procedure is mirrored on the left side, involving the opening of the left large ligament. The instrumentalist holds the scissors while the surgeon treats the cranial anex to update the posterior phase of the large ligament. Two lateral contiguous conduits are visible, resembling a gun barrel; the internal conduit corresponds to the ureter, and the external conduit corresponds to the internal iliac artery.

The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery, which is external to the uterine artery, and confirms its identity by pulling on it to check at the abdominal level. The uterine artery is clipped at its navel, with the ureter visible and under control. The artery is clipped lower than usual.

The surgeon identifies the uterine vein below the surgical field. A vertical incision is made on the uterus, approximately sixty to seventy percent of its volume, to remove a fibroma measuring seventy millimeters. Using bipolar forceps and scissors, the fibrous tracts binding the fibroma to the uterus are dissected and coagulated, ensuring coagulation is applied to the fibroma and not the myometrium. The fibroma is gradually separated from the myometrium without using energy, and hemostasis is achieved quickly with sutures.

The surgeon uses Bicryl No. I and Gumil-Carran sutures to invaginate the hysterotomy scar, incorporating part of the myometrium into the fibrous crater to minimize hematoma risk. The sutures create a fully peritoneal scar, reducing postoperative adhesion risk, especially on the posterior phase of the uterus. Atracorporeal nodes are used for precise tightening and tissue invasion within the crater, ensuring secure sutures.

The surgeon performs suturing on the hysterotomy scar, ensuring minimal entry and exit points to prevent excessive bleeding and avoid weakening the myometrial wall. The suture is adapted to the remaining volume on the biometer, with careful attention to preserving the uterine ligament and synaptic connections between the ovary and trunk, as the patient desires fertility.

The surgeon places a final stitch to secure the top of the pleat, carefully positioning the needle to avoid piercing the right and left tubular ostia. The number of stitches is deemed sufficient, and hemostasis is achieved without additional sutures.

The surgeon uses a chisel to shape the fibroma, placing it in the left foscilia and adjusting the speed to eight hundred turns per minute. The fibroma is carefully managed to prevent any debris from entering the pelvis. The ureter is visible and free, passing under the clipped uterine artery. The contralateral side is checked similarly, confirming the uterine artery has been clipped and the clips remain in place. The uterus is positioned inside, while the uterine artery is outside, and the area is washed.
